description:
Frequently asked questions - NotebookLM Help
[...]
# Frequently asked questions
[...]
Available features for the NotebookLM mobile app
[...]
The early version of the app includes many core features of NotebookLM and is designed to help you understand anything, anywhere. Learn more about the NotebookLM mobile app.
[...]
Unclear phrases: When your notebook contains many sources, NotebookLM retrieves the most relevant information based on your question, then builds a response from it. A specific question helps NotebookLM locate the most relevant pieces of information.
[...]
Limits for NotebookLM
[...]
With NotebookLM, you:
[...]
- Information not in sources: NotebookLM answers questions based on the information provided in your uploaded sources. If the answer isn't in the source material, it won't provide a response.
- Get 100 notebooks, with up to 50 sources each and 500,000 words each.
- Have daily limits of 50 chat queries and 3 audio generations.
[...]
File size limit for sources in NotebookLM
[...]
The current limit is 500,000 words per source or up to 200MB for local uploads. There’s no page limit.
[...]
How sources, chat context, and notes are used in prompts
[...]
- Notes: Only when you specifically select it.
- Sources: Always used in either the entire set or the subset you select.
- Conversation history: Used to generate responses.
[...]
If your source content is too short, NotebookLM references the entire document without a cited individual text from your source.

title: Generate Audio Overview in NotebookLM - NotebookLM Help
description:
Generate Audio Overview in NotebookLM - NotebookLM Help
[...]
# Generate Audio Overview in NotebookLM
[...]
Audio Overviews are deep-dive discussions between AI hosts that provide in-depth summaries of the key topics in your uploaded sources. These overviews are designed to be an objective reflection of your source content, rather than subjective opinions from the AI hosts.
[...]
## Generate an Audio Overview
[...]
Open an existing notebook or create a new one and upload sources.
[...]
To generate or delete an Audio Overview, you need edit access to a notebook.
[...]
In the "Studio" panel, select "Audio Overview" to generate. The audio will be generated in the background, so you can generate other artifacts concurrently or even navigate to other screens. Within the generation panel, you can also express preferences to customize the audio.
[...]
Choose a format.
[...]
- Deep Dive (default) for an in-depth conversation. Two hosts unpack and connect topics from your sources in a lively conversation.
- The Brief for a quick overview. A single speaker delivers the key takeaways of a document in under two minutes.
- The Critique for critical feedback. Two hosts provide a constructive evaluation of material like an essay or design doc.
- The Debate for exploring multiple perspectives. Two hosts engage in a formal, back-and-forth debate on the topic.
[...]
Input a prompt to provide instructions to focus on specific topics or adjust the expertise level.
[...]
- Audio Overviews, including voices, are AI-generated and may contain inaccuracies or audio glitches.
- NotebookLM may take a couple of minutes to generate the Audio Overview.
[...]
Tip: You can also listen to Audio Overviews while continuing to work within NotebookLM. You can explore relevant quotes, get citations, and query sources while listening to an Audio Overview in the background.
[...]
## Load a previous Audio Overview
[...]
Important: This is only applicable to notebooks that have previously generated Audio Overviews.
[...]
2. In
[...]
## Learn about interactive mode
[...]
You can join a conversation and interact with the AI hosts in Audio Overview. With your voice, you can ask the hosts for more details or to explain a concept differently.
[...]
## Interact with an Audio Overview
[...]
Important: The interactive mode experience is currently available only in English.
[...]
- The hosts will respond with a personalized answer based on your sources. After answering the question, they'll resume the original Audio Overview.
[...]
## Output language for Audio Overview
[...]
You can generate Audio Overviews in 80+ languages.
[...]
Supported languages for Audio Overview
[...]
- You can rate
[...]
discussion with Thumbs up or Thumbs down and provide feedback.

title: Use Mind Maps in NotebookLM - Google Help
description:
Use Mind Maps in NotebookLM - NotebookLM Help
[...]
# Use Mind Maps in NotebookLM
[...]
Important: The NotebookLM mobile app does not support this feature at this time. Learn more about the supported features in the NotebookLM mobile app.
[...]
Mind Maps in NotebookLM visually summarize your uploaded sources, showing main topics and related ideas as a branching diagram.
[...]
## When to use Mind Maps
[...]
- Get a quick overview: Understand the big picture and key themes of your source material quickly.
- Explore and learn new things easily: Get a clear entry point to new topics, making it easier to understand their main concepts before diving into the details.
- Connect the dots: Visually discover how different ideas relate to each other, and identify links and associations that might not be obvious.
- Organize your thoughts: Use Mind Maps to structure information, to improve how you understand and remember it.
[...]
## Generate a Mind Map
[...]
In NotebookLM, open an existing notebook.
[...]
- Or, create a new notebook and upload sources.
[...]
In the “Studio” panel, you can find a new note with your generated Mind Map.
[...]
## How to Interact with a Mind Map
[...]
Zoom in or out and scroll: This allows you to navigate different parts of the Mind Map and focus on specific areas.
[...]
Expand or collapse branches: You can expand branches to find more detailed sub-topics or collapse them to get a higher-level view.
[...]
Select nodes to ask questions: Directly select a node in the Mind Map to ask questions in the NotebookLM chat about that specific topic.
[...]
Other: To expand or collapse the view, download, or exit, look at the options in the top right corner.
[...]
## How to save & share a Mind Map
[...]
- Option #1: Generate a Mind Map, then share the entire notebook with another user. They will be able to load the same Mind Map in the Studio panel.
- Option #2: Download the Mind Map by selecting Download within the Mind Map window and share the downloaded file.
[...]
### Mind Map usage limits
[...]
Usage limits apply to Mind Map creation (subject to change):
[...]
- NotebookLM Standard: 10/day
- NotebookLM in Plus: 20/day
- NotebookLM in Pro: 100day
- NotebookLM in Ultra (20 TB Plan): 500/day
- NotebookLM in Ultra (30 TB Plan): 1,000/day
[...]
## Related resources
[...]

- Add or discover new sources for your Notebook
- Use chat in NotebookLM

Google Workspace Studio allows users to create "flows" that automate routine work tasks across Google Workspace apps, including Gmail, Drive, and Chat, and third-party services. Users with access to Gemini can build flows by describing what they want to do.
[...]
Flows can act on users’ behalf, drafting emails, creating and updating files, creating tasks, and more. Poorly configured flows could unintentionally edit or delete data, or send excessive notifications. Administrators can manage these risks through policy and monitoring.
[...]
This guide provides best practices for administrators to configure, support, and manage Workspace Studio to maximize productivity while maintaining security and control.
[...]
- Recommendation: Create a child organizational unit that has access to Studio turned off. This lets you quickly move a user to it to stop a flow they own.

- Allow users to use steps for Workspace services that they can access. Only block steps for a service if there’s a risk of abuse.
- Allow users who have access to Gemini for Google Workspace to use Gemini features in Studio.
[...]
- Action: Send a notification to the Super Admin.
- Condition: Create a rule where Step name contains "Ask Gemini", optionally, other AI-powered steps. For a list of all available steps, see Guide to Starters & Steps in Workspace Studio.
[...]

- Direct users to studio.workspace.google.com to create their first flow.
[...]
- Use case: Find the owner of an flow that runs an Ask Gemini step several times a minute.
- Filters: You can filter by Actor (user email), Flow ID, Event, Step name, and more.
- Data source: Select Rule log events.

If a flow is behaving erratically, you can stop it. You have a few options:
[...]
1. Contact Google Workspace support and provide the flow ID to have it stopped.
2. Use an investigation tool to get the Flow ID of the problematic flow.

description:
Manage access to Gemini features in Workspace services | Google Workspace with Gemini | Google Workspace Help

# Manage access to Gemini features in Workspace services

Supported editions for this feature:Enterprise Standard and Enterprise Plus; Teaching and Learning add-on and Education Plus; Google AI Pro for Education, and AI Ultra Access. Compare your edition

As an administrator, you can choose to enable or disable Gemini features and the side panel in the following Workspace services:

- Gmail
- Drive, Docs, Sheets, Slides, Forms, Drawings, and Vids
- Meet
- Chat
- Google Workspace Studio

The default setting for Gemini features in Workspace services is on. If you turn off features in a service, users can ask about an item in that service via Gemini in another service. For example, if you turn off Gemini in Drive and Docs, a user can ask Gemini in Gmail about a Sheet they own.

## Turn on or off Gemini in a Google service

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Generative AI Gemini for Workspace.

Go to Gemini for Workspace

Requires having the Gemini Settings administrator privilege.

Click the Feature access panel.

Note: Available only with these editions.

(Optional) To apply the setting only to some users, at the side, select an organizational unit (often used for departments) or configuration group (advanced).

Group settings override organizational units. Learn more

Click Edit next to the service you want to enable or disable.

Click On or Off, and then click Save.

Note: To access Gemini in Workspace services, users need to turn on smart features and personalization.

### What other controls are available?

- You can turn access to the Gemini app (gemini.google.com) on or off. (Note: this has no effect on other AI features in Google Workspace services.)
- You can turn off NotebookLM on or off as an additional service.
- You can turn access to Google Vids on or off.
- You can control whether users let Google AI take notes in meetings.
- You can turn Google Workspace smart features on or off.
- Users can control smart features in Google products.
- You can turn Gemini for App Creation or Gemini in AppSheet Solutions on or off.
- You can turn Workspace Studio on or off. You can also block Gemini from Workspace Studio with API controls.

title: Allow or block Gemini for Google Workspace steps | Studio
description:
Allow or block Gemini for Google Workspace steps | Studio | Google Workspace Help

# Allow or block Gemini for Google Workspace steps

Supported editions for this feature: Enterprise Standard and Enterprise Plus; Teaching and Learning add-on and Education Plus; Google AI Pro for Education, and AI Ultra Access. Compare your edition

Users designated as under 18 can't use AI features in Workspace Studio.

As an administrator, you can control whether Gemini for Google Workspace is turned on for Google Workspace Studio. With Gemini, users can create custom AI steps from a prompt and use these steps in their flows.

If you want to block only certain AI-powered steps, you can use the setting in Allow or block Workspace service–specific steps.

Note: AI features in Workspace Studio follow the privacy principles in the Generative AI in Google Workspace Privacy Hub.

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Generative AI Gemini for Workspace.

Go to Gemini for Workspace

Requires having the Gemini Settings administrator privilege.

(Optional) To apply the setting only to some users, at the side, select an organizational unit (often used for departments) or configuration group (advanced).

Group settings override organizational units. Learn more

1. Click the Feature access panel.
2. Next to Workspace Studio, click Edit.
3. Click On or Off.
4. Click Save. Or, you might click Override for an organizational unit. To later restore the inherited value, click Inherit (or Unset for a group).

When Gemini for Google Workspace is turned off for Workspace Studio, people can't do the following:

- Create flows from a prompt.
- Use AI steps. Any active flows that have AI steps in them stop working.

title: Set up activity alerts for Workspace Studio  |  Google Workspace Help
description:
Set up activity alerts for Workspace Studio | Google Workspace Help

# Set up activity alerts for Workspace Studio

As an administrator, you can get alerts about Google Workspace Studio flow activity, such as when flows run a lot or use a lot of AI steps. You set alerts in the Workspace Alert Center and can be notified by email or in the Admin Console.

This page describes how to set up 2 common alerts.

## Example 1: Get alerts for high flow activity

You can set up an activity rule to notify administrators when flow run an unusually high number of times within a short period. These alerts help you quickly identify potential loops, misconfigurations, or quota consumption issues.

This example shows how to create an activity rule that sends a notification when the "Start run" event occurs more than 100 times in one hour.

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Rules.

Requires having the View Trust Rules administrator privilege.

Add a condition with the following values:

- Attribute: Select Event.
- Operator: Leave as Is.
- Value: Select Start run.

Configure the frequency threshold values:

- Count: Enter 100.
- Time period: Select 1 Hour.
- This defines the condition as "more than 100 times in 1 hour".

Verify the settings:

- Source: Workspace Studio log events
- Conditions: Event Is Start run
- Threshold: If the event happens more than 100 times in 1 hour
- Alerts: On (if you chose to get notifications in the Alert center)
- Emails: On (if you chose to send emails)
- Rule status: Active

## Example 2: Get alerts when flows use Gemini a lot

You can set up an activity rule to notify administrators when Workspace Studio flows use the Ask Gemini step. These alerts help you to track how often flows use this AI-powered step.

This example shows how to create an activity rule that sends a notification when these AI-powered steps are used more than 1000 times in 24 hours.

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Rules.

Requires having the View Trust Rules administrator privilege.

Add a condition with the following values:

- Attribute: Select Event.
- Operator: Leave as Is.
- Value: Select Start step.

Click Add condition and set up another as follows:

- Attribute: Select Step name.
- Operator: Select Contains.
- Value: Enter "Ask Gemini".

Verify the settings:

- Source: Workspace Studio log events
- Conditions: Event Is Start step AND Step Name Contains Ask Gemini
- Threshold: If the event happens more than 1000 times in 24 hours
- Alerts: On (if you chose to get notifications in the Alert center)
- Emails: On (if you chose to send emails)
- Rule status: Active

title: Stop a Workspace Studio flow as an admin
description:
Stop a Workspace Studio flow as an admin | Google Workspace Help

# Stop a Workspace Studio flow as an admin

With Google Workspace Studio, people in your organization can create flows to automate work across Workspace and third-party services they're allowed to connect to. These flows can make people more productive by automating routine tasks, but they can sometimes work in unexpected or unwanted ways.

You usually discover unwanted flow activity from reports from users or in Workspace event logs, such as:

- High frequency activity—For example, more messages sent in Gmail or Google Chat, or more changes to a file, or more files created than normal or possible for a human being. Tip: Chat messages sent by Studio flows have a chip next to the sender's name showing they were sent by a Studio flow.
- Unwanted activity that happens on a regular schedule—For example, it might repeat approximately every hour at all times of day. Note: Flows have built-in stagger so the interval may not be exactly the same for each repeat.

To stop a flow, first you figure out which flow is causing the problem, then send a request to Support to stop it.

## Step 1: Find which flow is causing the problem

Follow the instructions in Workspace Studio log events to search the Studio logs using the Audit and investigation tool or Security investigation tool. Try the following attribute filters:

1. Actor—Set as the user's email address.
2. Event—If you know when the problem occurred, filter by Step complete and look for a step that finished at about the same time. You might also be able to filter by Step app if you know the activity happened in Gmail, Chat, or another Workspace app.

When you find the flow, in the results list, make a note of the Flow ID.

## Step 2: Stop a user's flow

If you need to stop a flow immediately and can't wait for support, you can stop all of the owner's flows.

Important: These steps turn off all the user's flows. Make sure that they don't have any business critical flows before you continue.

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Apps Google Workspace Workspace Studio.

Go to Workspace Studio

Requires having the Service Settings administrator privilege.

1. Add an organizational unit. Set the user's current organizational unit as the parent.
2. Move the user to the new organizational unit.
3. Click Service status.
4. At the left, select the new organizational unit.
5. Change Service status to Off.
6. Click Override.
7. After the flow is stopped by Support, you can move the user back to their original organizational unit so they can use Workspace Studio again. The flow remains off until the user turns it back on.

title: Manage access to Gemini features in Workspace services
description:
Manage access to Gemini features in Workspace services | Generative AI | Google Workspace Help

# Manage access to Gemini features in Workspace services

Supported editions for this feature: Enterprise Standard and Enterprise Plus; Teaching and Learning add-on and Education Plus; Google AI Pro for Education, and AI Ultra Access. Compare your edition

As an administrator, you can choose to enable or disable Gemini features and the side panel in the following Workspace services:

- Gmail
- Calendar
- Drive, Docs, Sheets, Slides, Forms, Drawings, and Vids
- Meet
- Chat
- Google Workspace Studio

The default setting for Gemini features in Workspace services is on.

Even if Gemini is off for a specific app, users can still access its data when using Gemini in other apps. For example, a user in Gmail can ask Gemini about a Drive file when Gemini is turned off for Drive.

## Turn on or off Gemini in a Google service

In the Google Admin console, go to Menu Generative AI Gemini for Workspace.

Go to Gemini for Workspace

Requires having the Gemini Settings administrator privilege.

Click the Feature access panel.

Note: Available only with these editions.

(Optional) To apply the setting only to some users, at the side, select an organizational unit (often used for departments) or configuration group (advanced).

Group settings override organizational units. Learn more

Click Edit next to the service you want to enable or disable.

Click On or Off, and then click Save.

Note: To access Gemini in Workspace services, users need to turn on smart features and personalization.

### What other controls are available?

- You can turn access to the Gemini app (gemini.google.com) on or off. (Note: this has no effect on other AI features in Google Workspace services.)
- You can turn off NotebookLM on or off as an additional service.
- You can turn access to Google Vids on or off.
- You can control whether users let Google AI take notes in meetings.
- You can turn Google Workspace smart features on or off.
- Users can control smart features in Google products.
- You can turn Gemini for App Creation or Gemini in AppSheet Solutions on or off.
- You can turn Workspace Studio on or off. You can also block Gemini from Workspace Studio with API controls.

title: Create a flow from scratch - Workspace Studio Help
description:
Create a flow from scratch - Workspace Studio Help

# Create a flow from scratch

To create a fully custom automation, you can build a flow from scratch:

Optional: Before you turn on your flow, to make sure it works properly, click Test run Start.

- This runs the flow immediately so you can verify the output.
- If everything works fine, you get a "Run Completed" message.

Every flow has 2 main parts:

Steps: These are the tasks the flow performs after it starts. You can add multiple steps to a flow. A step could be:

- “Draft a reply.”
- “Notify me in Chat.”

Tip: Variables are placeholders for information from your starter or previous steps. They let you create dynamic and powerful flows.

- For example, you can use a variable to insert the subject line of an email from your starter into a Google Chat message step. Learn how to use variables to pass data in your flow.

## Build your flow from scratch with an example

In this example, you'll create a flow from scratch that automatically drafts a reply in Gmail whenever you receive an email that contains the word 'Invoice'.

### Step 1: Choose your starter

Enter:`Invoice`

- This tells the flow to run only when an email contains the word "Invoice" in its subject or body.

For a full list of all available starters and steps, go to Guide to Starters and Steps in Workspace Studio.

### Step 2: Add a step

1. Below your starter, click Choose a step.
2. Under the "Gmail" category, select Draft a reply.

### Step 3: Use variables to create a dynamic message

Type:`I will review it and get back to you.`

Your message should look like this:

`Hello,`

`I received the invoice regarding:` [Email subject]

`I will review it and get back to you.`

### Step 4: Name, test & turn on your flow

At the bottom, click Test run.

- A new window will show that allows you to select a recent email from your inbox that matches your starter conditions to test the flow with.

In the "Email received" field, enter:`Invoice`

This finds recent emails that match your starter conditions.

Select an email.

Click Start.

- If everything works fine, you get a "Run Completed" message and should find a new draft email in your Gmail Drafts folder.

You've now built a smart and custom flow from scratch that filters your email and keeps your team informed in real time.

## Related resources

- When you're finished, click Turn on.
- Create a flow with Gemini AI
- Create a flow from a template
- Manage and share your flows

# Take actions in third-party services with flows
[...]
You can have the flows you set up in Google Workspace Studio do tasks in third-party services using integrations. Integrations let your flows work across Google Workspace apps and other apps like Asana, Mailchimp, and Salesforce. Many integrations also work in Gemini Apps.
[...]
## Use third-party integration steps in flows
[...]
Integration steps are available wherever you create and edit flows, including:
[...]
- When you create a flow with AI.
- When you create a flow with a template that already has an integration step.
- In the step selector in the editor. Scroll down to the section with the name of the third-party service.
[...]
1. Go to https://studio.workspace.google.com and create a flow.
2. In the left panel, click the integration step.
3. If you haven’t connected it yet, click Connect. Follow the on-screen instructions to let the integration access your Google Account data and finish connecting. You might need to install a helper app from the third-party service. If you have trouble connecting, learn how to troubleshoot integration steps.
[...]
4. Set up the step.
5. (Optional) To try your flow, click Test run. A test run runs your flow once, taking real actions so you can see the outcome. Select the starting conditions, then click Start. Learn more about test runs.
[...]
6. Click Turn on.
[...]
## Connect third-party
[...]
1. Go to https://workspace.google.com/marketplace/category/integrations. This link takes you to a pre-filtered view of only integrations. Most work with Workspace Studio.
2. When you find one you want to connect to, click it.
3. Click Connect.
4. Follow the on-screen instructions to let the integration access your Google Account data and finish connecting. You might need to install a helper app from the third-party service. If you have trouble connecting, learn how to troubleshoot integration steps.
[...]
When you go to Workspace Studio and add a step from that third-party service, you won't have to connect again.
[...]
To connect to the integration again, add an integration step to a flow and go through the connection process.
[...]
4. Integrations need you to grant permissions to connect Workspace Studio and the third-party service. For some, like HubSpot, you select the permissions individually. If a permission is missing, such as to get contact data or create a contact, then the step that needs that permission won't work. Reconnect to the third-party services and grant all permissions. If you continue to see error messages, your Google Workspace administrator might block some permissions. Reach out to them for help.

# Best practices for using AI in automations
[...]
To improve the results when using AI in automations, apply the following best practices:
[...]
### Should I use AI in automations?
[...]
As an app creator, you can incorporate predefined AI capabilities as tasks in your automations. These AI tasks rely on Google’s Gemini models. You don’t need to train them, but that means they aren't tailored to your data.
[...]
To figure out if AI tasks are a good fit, we encourage you to try them out, first on a few examples and then on more data.
[...]
- If your AI task does not require obscure knowledge, it might help you
- If the required knowledge to properly get the right response is difficult to find online, then your AI task might not return good results; for example, tasks that require industry-specific knowledge might impact the quality of the results
[...]
### Have a
[...]
and handle unexpected behavior
[...]
Set up your automations to use AI and human expertise in a collaborative manner to ensure the accuracy of the results, by doing the following:
[...]
1. Make sure humans can quickly review AI-generated results in your app.
2. Add steps in the automation flow to handle unexpected behavior from AI tasks.
[...]
## Best practices when building apps using AI
[...]
Each AI task has an Additional details field. AppSheet recommends that you use this field to provide more information about the task and the expected results.
[...]
For the columns that are used to save or temporarily store output from an AI task, AppSheet recommends that you add column descriptions to provide more information for Gemini to use in its data evaluation. See Configure column properties.
[...]
Don't use expressions in column descriptions that are intended to be used with AI tasks in automations. The expressions in a column description won't be evaluated and might impact the quality of the results.
[...]

title: Conversational Analytics in Looker overview  |  Google Cloud Documentation
description:
Conversational Analytics is a chat-with-your-data feature that is powered by Gemini for Google Cloud. Grounded in the Looker semantic modeling layer, Conversational Analytics empowers users in your organization to ask data-related questions in regular, natural (conversational) language for governed, trusted self-service BI. This approach accelerates adoption of analytics across your organization by providing enterprise-grade governance and security.
[...]
Conversational Analytics is available in both Looker (Google Cloud core) instances and in Looker (original) instances.
[...]
Conversational Analytics uses Gemini for Google Cloud to interpret natural language questions and provide answers based on your data in Looker. It uses the Looker semantic model — the LookML definitions of your data — as its source of truth to ensure that responses are accurate and consistent. Conversational Analytics can interpret your business definitions for metrics like "revenue" or "churn" because they are defined in LookML, using these definitions to deliver accurate and consistent questions.
[...]
uses built-in Gemini capabilities to interpret and summarize results.
[...]
- It uses Python
[...]
execution through the Code Interpreter to perform further analysis on the results.
[...]
- It creates visualizations based on query
[...]
- Set up Conversational Analytics in Looker: Enable Conversational Analytics for a Looker instance and grant access to users.
[...]
Conversational Analytics is not yet included in FedRAMP High or FedRAMP Medium authorization boundaries. Prior to enabling the Gemini in Looker setting for your Looker instance, discuss with your authorizing body whether Gemini for Google Cloud's compliance offerings meet the needs of your organization.
[...]
For Looker (Google Cloud core) instances, each Assured Workloads control package that becomes available will add Gemini in Looker features as default offerings as that package's change requirements and processes are met. Conversational Analytics in Looker respects the compliance capabilities of the associated Looker (Google Cloud core) instance, with the following exception:
[...]
associated with Convers

# Recommended setup and rollout strategy for Conversational Analytics in Looker
[...]
This guide is intended to help plan the rollout of Conversational Analytics to users of a Looker instance. It recommends the following phased approach for implementing Conversational Analytics in Looker:
[...]
- Phase 1: Curate data and define the initial scope
- Phase 2: Configure agents and validate internally
- Phase 3: Expand Conversational Analytics adoption to more users
[...]
This approach lets you start with a small, controlled scope, validate your setup, and then expand to more users and data.
[...]
In this phase, prepare your data for users to query with Conversational Analytics and define the scope of the initial deployment. Follow these recommendations for starting with a small and controlled scope:
[...]
- Initial setup: Follow the setup steps in Set up Conversational Analytics in Looker for your instance.
[...]
- Limit initial user access: To enable internal testing and validation, use Looker's permission system to grant the required permissions to use Conversational Analytics to a small set of users who are familiar with the data. To start, consider limiting access to one or two models that you have curated for Conversational Analytics.
[...]
- Select curated Explores: Start with one or two well-structured Explores that are based on relatively clean data and that provide clear business value. Optimize these Explores for Conversational Analytics in Looker by following the detailed instructions in Best practices for configuring Conversational Analytics in Looker.
[...]
## Phase
[...]
and validate internally
[...]
In this phase, build and refine your Conversational Analytics agents, and then thoroughly test them with internal users to confirm accuracy and effectiveness. This phase involves the following steps:
[...]
## Phase 3: Expand Conversational Analytics adoption to more users
[...]
In this phase, expand Conversational Analytics adoption to more users by granting access, collecting feedback, and iterating on your agents. This phase involves the following steps:
[...]
Expand access: Once the agents prove stable and valuable, expand access to other relevant user groups and introduce new curated agents by granting these users the appropriate permissions. You can also introduce new curated agents and expand access to the models that are available to the Gemini role, following the same processes that were used in the previous phases.
